context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |

Course context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Investigation Papers

21A.157 The Meaning of Life
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Weight: 60% of the final grade (3 papers × 20% each)

Overview:
Students will complete three (3) Investigation Papers (1500–1600 words each). Five of the subject modules we cover offer two options for Investigation Papers. Pick three modules on which to write, and for each select one Investigation option. Plan in advance which Investigations you intend to do, since some involve coordinating with other people or travel to off-campus destinations. Each assignment requires collecting anthropological data using methods such as participant observation or interviewing, and then analyzing your findings through the lens of related readings. You must cite, quote, and engage relevant readings from the class in your papers.


Module 1: Family
Due: Session 6

Option 1:
Interview someone who is or has been married about their expectations and experiences of the widespread yet culturally varied social institution of marriage. In what ways do their experiences match or diverge from their expectations before being married? To what extent have they experienced marriage as a "moral laboratory" (refer to Cheryl Mattingly's article "Moral Selves and Moral Scenes: Narrative Experiments in Everyday Life")? In their view, how has marriage or "being married" changed over time, from (say) their parents' generation to their own? What have been the gains and losses of these changes, for whom? If the interviewee is an immigrant or the child of immigrants, how does cultural difference factor into their experience and assessment? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Write an Op-Ed in (at least partial) response to Amy Chua's "Why Chinese Mothers are Superior." You may argue against her piece or write in praise of it — either way, your piece should speak from the perspective of a parent's child. You do not need to speak directly from your own personal experience (although you may) but rather from that of your generation more broadly — think of your friends' experiences, of discussions you've seen unfold over social media, perhaps. Be sure to engage the insights of and cite at least two other readings (Hirsch, Kuan, Mattingly, Kolbert) in your discussion.


Module 2: Wealth
Due: Session 10

Option 1:
Interview someone about what money means to them. How is it connected with independence or interdependence? How do they think about earning money? About sharing, lending, or giving money? Do they receive money from their family, send money to their family, or some combination? Does money imply freedom or servitude? What is money good for? Has their relationship to money changed over time? Is money a source of pride or shame? What does debt mean to them? Do they view money differently from other people in their family (particularly people of other generations) or their peers? Do they feel like their views about money are in line with the rest of society? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Interview someone about their job search or employment history. This could be someone who is currently employed, unemployed, or even retired. In addition to documenting past and present jobs and future career aspirations, explore what work means to this person. Do they live to work or work to live? To what extent does their sense of identity or personal worth come from work? Do they fantasize about changing jobs? How much did they train for their current position? Are they satisfied with their current situation (in terms of responsibilities, opportunities, relationships with co-workers, remuneration, etc.)? Why or why not? Is their work adequately appreciated by others? Why or why not? Is the job getting better or worse? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.


Module 3: Sex
Due: Session 14

Option 1:
Find and analyze a piece (or pieces) of dating and/or sex advice: this could be from the past or the present; something you deem as helpful or harmful. Who's the presumed audience for this advice (and who's left out)? What is presumed about what 'sex' is and why it's important — and to whom? What broader values or virtues — e.g., about human nature, about pleasure, about gender (being "good" at being a man or woman), about morality (being a "good" person) — are encoded, implicitly or explicitly, in the advice given? What do you find helpful and/or harmful about this advice, to whom, and why? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Write a dating and/or sex advice column for a specific audience of your choosing (be sure to specify). Keeping your target audience in mind, what do you think they ought better to understand about what sex is (and is not), what it means (culturally, morally, legally, etc.), and what its rightful place should be in people's (again, be specific) lives. Think about the best way to convey this message to your audience (through anecdotal illustration, in terms of tone, etc.). Explain your reasoning as you go. Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion, either as part of your main text, or in footnotes, or as a coda.


Module 4: Self-Cultivation
Due: Session 19

Option 1:
Conduct a week-long self-tracking exercise with the aid of an app (e.g. time management, dietary intake, exercise, sleep or mood tracking). Describe what you did and why you chose the activity/app you did. Analyze not only the data that resulted, but also the experience of tracking. What surprised you about the exercise, and/or results? Did awareness of tracking affect your behavior or thoughts at the time, regardless of the data? What benefits, or losses, did you experience, or do you think would accrue over time if you were to continue the practice? Will you try to continue? Why or why not? In analyzing your findings, draw on at least two readings from this module (and not only the media pieces assigned for recitation).

Option 2:
Interview a friend or relative about a hobby or avocation. If possible, accompany them to engage in the activity and, ideally, try to do it with them. How important is the hobby to their sense of self? Has involvement with the activity changed them over time and, if so, how? What kinds of qualities are necessary to excel at the activity, and do those qualities translate into other aspects of life? Is there a community associated with this practice and, if so, how important is membership in that community to your interviewee? Does the hobby help to relieve stress or does it have stresses of its own? In the context of this activity, are there accomplishments that the person is particularly proud of? How important is challenging oneself to keeping the activity fun or gratifying? In analyzing your findings, draw on at least two readings from this module.

Student Example:
"Self-Cultivation Investigation Paper - Option 2" (PDF)


Module 5: Belonging
Due: Session 23

Option 1:
Visit a cultural institution or historical site associated with collective cultural memory that raises questions concerning belonging (e.g., Boston's Museum of African American History and/or Black Heritage Trail, the Jewish Heritage Museum, the West End immigration museum — you might also look to see if there are current art exhibits or other relevant cultural events to attend). What is the central narrative about belonging (and/or exclusion) presented at the site or exhibit? Whom does the site seem to address as its audience(s)? If interpretative materials are available, what do they say about the meaning and significance of the site or event commemorated, and to whom? How does the site, or the history to which it speaks, connect to the social and physical environment around it today? You may wish to conduct this site visit with a friend who has a meaningful personal connection with the place or history evoked; if so, how does this affect your own experience? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Identify an object that represents a sense of belonging — to a particular place, an ethnic group, a religion, a nation, etc. (an entity broader than 'family') — for yourself or someone you know. (If the object is yours, offer your own reflections in this assignment; if the object is someone else's, interview the owner to address the following questions.) What is the history of the object, and how did its owner come to acquire it? What stories does it contain? To what larger group or history or community does it connect the owner? Where does the object reside in its owner's room/home (e.g., is it hidden away or put openly on display)? What can we learn from this object and its personal significance to its owner about the cultural significance and politics of belonging more broadly? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Student Example:
"Belonging Investigation Paper - Option 2" (PDF)

Note: Student examples appear courtesy of MIT students and are anonymous by request.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m trying to figure out my Investigation Paper for 21A.157. I feel a little stuck choosing a module/option and turning it into a focused argument instead of just a summary. Could you help me narrow one down?
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! Great that you're thinking ahead. Before we narrow anything down — which of the five modules (Family, Wealth, Sex, Self-Cultivation, Belonging) feels most connected to something you're already curious about or have easy access to people/places for? That's usually the best starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning of the process - choosing a module and option. Rather than guiding them toward a specific choice, I should ask them a question that helps them reflect on what they already have access to (people to interview, places to visit, activities to track) and what genuinely interests them. This is a light Socratic opener that puts the ownership of the choice on them.

exchanges.student: Probably Self-Cultivation or Wealth. Self-Cultivation seems easier logistically, but I’m not sure how to make the analysis anthropological and not just “here’s what happened in my week.” Wealth also seems interesting, but interviewing someone well feels harder.
exchanges.tutor: You're already asking exactly the right question. So let me push on it a bit: what do you think would make the difference between a personal diary entry and an anthropological analysis of the same experience? What's the ingredient that transforms one into the other?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a real tension: they're drawn to Self-Cultivation for logistical reasons but are already asking the right analytical question about how to move beyond mere description. This is a good sign. Rather than resolving the tension for them, I should probe their thinking about what 'anthropological analysis' means to them in this context - this will help them self-discover that the readings are the key bridge between raw data and analysis. I'll keep it tight and pointed.

exchanges.student: I think it’s not just describing what I did, but connecting it to bigger ideas from the readings? Maybe also noticing what values or assumptions the app builds in, instead of treating the numbers as neutral. I’m not fully sure though.
exchanges.tutor: Those are both sharp observations — especially the second one. The idea that a tool encodes values is a really productive analytical lens. Now, have you looked at the actual readings assigned for the Self-Cultivation module? What themes do you see there that might connect to what you just said?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually landed on two strong insights: using readings to connect to bigger ideas, and interrogating the values embedded in the app/technology itself. This is genuinely good thinking and deserves acknowledgment. Rather than elaborating further for them, I should affirm and then push them to connect this insight back to the actual assignment prompt and readings, nudging them toward committing to a module.

exchanges.student: Not carefully enough, honestly. I vaguely remember themes like discipline, self-improvement, maybe optimization, but that’s still kind of blurry for me. Could you help me turn those into a possible paper question?
exchanges.tutor: That's actually the step you need to take before we can get to a paper question — go back to at least two of the Self-Cultivation readings and look for a concept or argument that surprises you or pushes back against your intuitions. What do the authors say about self-tracking or self-improvement that you wouldn't have expected? Come back with that and we'll have something to work with.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to do the intellectual work of formulating a research question for them, which I should not do. Instead, I should redirect them to the readings first - they've admitted they haven't read carefully enough, and that's the right place to start. A focused question naturally emerges from engaging with the texts. I'll be direct about this without being harsh.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that makes sense. I’m a little annoyed because I wanted to get moving, but I think my problem is I’m trying to outline before I know the concepts. I’ll pull two readings and look for a claim about discipline/optimization/selfhood that feels non-obvious, then bring that back.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — and that frustration is actually a sign you're thinking clearly about the process. Go find those non-obvious claims and bring them back. We'll build from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has shown good self-awareness and identified exactly the right next step on their own. This deserves brief acknowledgment. No need to add scaffolding - they've got a clear task. I'll affirm and send them off efficiently.

exchanges.student: Got it. I’ll do that and then ask for help connecting the claims to a paper argument.
exchanges.tutor: Sounds good. See you when you're back with the readings.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a clear plan and is signing off to do the reading. A brief, encouraging send-off is appropriate. No need to add anything substantive.

exchanges.student: Thanks — I’ll come back once I’ve got a couple of concepts instead of just a topic.
exchanges.tutor: Perfect — that's exactly the right distinction. See you then.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrapping up this exchange with a clear and correct understanding of what they need to do. A brief acknowledgment is all that's needed.

exchanges.student: Hi again — I went back to the Self-Cultivation readings, and I think one idea is that self-tracking isn’t just measuring me, it kind of trains me to see myself in a certain way. Another is that improvement can start feeling moralized, like being “good” means being disciplined/productive. I’m not sure if that’s too vague, but I think that’s the direction.
exchanges.tutor: Good — those are both substantive observations, not just topics. Now, do you see a relationship between those two ideas? How might they connect to each other in the context of actually doing the tracking exercise the assignment asks for?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has returned with two genuinely strong conceptual observations drawn from the readings, which is exactly what I asked for. This is good progress and deserves acknowledgment. Now the task is to help them see how these two ideas might connect to each other and to the specific assignment prompt - particularly the self-tracking exercise (Option 1). I should ask a question that pushes them to think about the relationship between the two concepts, rather than treating them as separate. I should not synthesize the ideas for them.

exchanges.student: I think they connect because the app doesn’t just record behavior — it pushes me to judge behavior. So if I track sleep or time use, I might start treating certain patterns as virtuous and others as failure, even if that framing came from the app and not fully from me. Is that getting closer?
exchanges.tutor: That's a real argument, not just an observation — you've identified an agency question hiding inside a self-improvement tool. Now try to put it in the form of a question your paper could investigate. What would you actually be asking?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just articulated a genuinely strong analytical claim - that the app mediates and moralizes self-perception. This is the kind of insight that can anchor a paper argument. I should affirm this clearly, then push them to sharpen it into an actual paper question or thesis direction, which is what they came back to do. I should not write the question for them but ask them to try formulating it themselves.

exchanges.student: Maybe: how does a self-tracking app shape my sense of what counts as a good use of time, instead of just helping me observe it? Or maybe that’s too narrow? I’m trying to make it specific without making it trivial.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong question — specific enough to be answerable, but the implications are broad. The real test now: which specific claims from your two readings would you use to back it up, complicate it, or push it further? Try mapping that out.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has formulated a solid paper question and is now second-guessing its scope. The question is actually good - specific but with real analytical depth. I should affirm this and push them toward thinking about how the readings would substantiate or complicate the question, which is the next concrete step. I want to avoid them spiraling on whether the question is 'right' before they've tested it against the texts.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout the conversation, consistently refusing to give the student the answer and instead prompting them to derive insights themselves. The student arrived at a genuine research question through their own reasoning, guided by well-placed questions. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided a near-submission-ready answer, never rewrote student text, and never stated the key idea before the student reasoned toward it. Every turn pushed the student to generate the next step themselves — the research question in Turn 10 was entirely student-derived. 1.2: The tutor built on each student contribution (e.g., affirming the 'values encoded in tools' insight in Turn 3, then scaffolding toward readings), and diagnosed the student's confusion (trying to outline before knowing concepts) before redirecting. 1.3: The tutor gave explicit process-level feedback, notably in Turn 5 ('you're trying to outline before you know the concepts') and Turn 4 (directing the student to find non-obvious claims before attempting a paper question). 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation progressed steadily without repetition or drift; the tutor kept the student anchored to the assignment throughout. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise, well-targeted, and consistently warm and encouraging without being evaluative or grade-implying.
